# Provenance Guard 🛡️

A backend a creative-sharing platform can plug in to answer one question honestly:
**was this text written by a human, or generated by AI and passed off as human?**
Not to police creativity — to protect attribution, communicate uncertainty instead of
forcing a binary, and give creators a way to appeal.

**Author:** Jaed Pizarro · CodePath AI201 · Project 4
**Stack:** Flask · Groq (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) · pure-Python stylometry · Flask-Limiter · JSON audit log

---

## Architecture overview — the path a submission takes

`POST /submit` → **rate limiter** → mint `content_id` → **detection pipeline** (3
independent signals) → **confidence scoring** (weighted average → calibrated `p_ai`) →
**verdict** via asymmetric thresholds → **transparency label** (+ Verified-Human badge
if the creator is credentialed) → write **submission record + structured audit-log
entry** → JSON response.

The **appeal flow** (`POST /appeal`) takes a `content_id` + the creator's reasoning,
flips the item to `under_review`, logs the appeal beside the original decision, and
returns a confirmation for a human reviewer (`GET /log`). Full ASCII diagram + narrative
live in [`planning.md`](planning.md#2-architecture).

## Detection signals — multi-signal by design

Three genuinely distinct signals (2 required; the 3rd is the *ensemble* stretch). Each
measures a different **property** of the text, so together they are more informative
than any one alone.

| Signal | What it measures | Why I chose it | What it **misses** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Groq LLM** (semantic) | Whether the text *reads* like an assistant — holistic coherence & generic phrasing | Captures meaning-level cues no heuristic can | LLM detectors are imperfect; lightly-edited AI fools them |
| **Stylometric** (structural) | Sentence-length **burstiness**, vocabulary evenness, punctuation/casing **regularity**. AI is uniform; humans are bursty & messy | Cheap, transparent, no API, genuinely independent of the LLM | A careful human essayist writing uniformly looks AI-like |
| **Lexical** (surface) | Density of LLM "tell" connectives (`furthermore`, `it is important to note`, `stakeholders`…) + contraction rate | Fast, interpretable, catches classic AI phrasing | Formal human academics use the same connectives → capped & down-weighted |

They are combined as a **weighted average** — LLM `0.50`, stylometric `0.30`, lexical
`0.20`. If the LLM signal is unavailable (no key / network error) its weight is
redistributed to the heuristics and the result is flagged `degraded: true` so the label
and log stay honest. (The multimodal image path swaps in a metadata signal — see below.)

## Confidence scoring — how it's combined & validated

The system reports a single **`p_ai`** = probability the content is AI-generated
(`0` = confident human, `1` = confident AI). Verdicts come from **asymmetric
thresholds**, because on a writing platform *wrongly branding a human as AI is worse
than missing some AI*:

| `p_ai` | Verdict |
|---|---|
| `≥ 0.72` | **Likely AI** (high bar to accuse) |
| `0.40 – 0.72` | **Uncertain** (wide benefit-of-the-doubt band; 0.5 lives here) |
| `≤ 0.40` | **Likely human** (easier bar to clear) |

**Validation — the scores vary meaningfully and reach all three bands.** Measured on
the spec's four calibration inputs with all three signals active (Groq LLM +
stylometric + lexical):

| Input | `p_ai` | Verdict | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clearly AI ("…transformative paradigm shift… stakeholders…") | **0.81** | Likely AI | 81% |
| Clearly human ("ok so i finally tried that ramen place…") | **0.17** | Likely human | 83% |
| Borderline — formal human (monetary-policy essay) | **0.66** | Uncertain | — |
| Borderline — lightly-edited AI (remote-work musing) | **0.41** | Uncertain | — |

**Two submissions with noticeably different confidence** (required):
a **high-confidence** case — the clearly-AI paragraph scored **`p_ai = 0.81` → "Likely
AI", 81% confidence** (per-signal: LLM 0.80, stylometric 0.71, lexical 1.0) — versus a
**lower-confidence** case — the lightly-edited AI text scored **`p_ai = 0.41` →
"Uncertain"**. Same system, a 0.40 spread in the score and two
different labels: the score is meaningful, not a constant. The two borderline inputs
correctly land in *Uncertain* rather than being falsely branded, which is the
false-positive safeguard working as designed.

## Transparency label — the three variants (exact text)

The label the reader sees changes with the score (never constant). `{conf}` is a 0–100
confidence in the stated verdict; `{p_ai}` is the AI-likelihood percentage.

| Variant | Exact text |
|---|---|
| **High-confidence AI** | ⚠️ Likely AI-generated. Our automated analysis found strong signals of machine authorship in this text (confidence {conf}%). This is an estimate, not a certainty — no AI detector is perfect. The creator can appeal this label. |
| **High-confidence human** | ✅ Likely human-written. Our automated analysis found little evidence of AI generation (confidence {conf}%). Attribution estimates can be wrong; this label reflects an automated check, not a guarantee. |
| **Uncertain** | ❓ Attribution uncertain. Our analysis could not confidently tell whether this was written by a person or by AI (estimated {p_ai}% likelihood of AI). We're showing this openly instead of guessing — treat authorship as unknown. |

A credentialed creator's label is prefixed with:
`🔵 Verified Human Creator — this creator completed Provenance Guard identity attestation.`

## Rate limiting — chosen limits & reasoning

`POST /submit` is limited to **`10 per minute; 100 per day`** (per client IP).
**Reasoning:** a genuine writer submits their own work a handful of times an hour —
10/min and 100/day never touch real use — while a script trying to flood the detector
(or run up the LLM bill) is stopped fast. The two-window pair blocks both bursts and
slow all-day abuse.

**Evidence** — 12 rapid requests against the live limit:

```
Status codes: [200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 200, 429, 429]
-> 10 x 200 (accepted), 2 x 429 (rate-limited)
429 body: "429 Too Many Requests — 10 per 1 minute"
```

## Audit log — structured, queryable

Every decision, appeal, and verification is appended to `data/audit_log.json` (JSON,
not print statements) and surfaced at `GET /log`. A submission entry logs timestamp,
content_id, creator, content_type, verdict, confidence, `p_ai`, and **each individual
signal's score**. Real entries from a test run (submission + its appeal):

```json
[
  {
    "timestamp": "2026-07-01T04:46:28+00:00",
    "event": "submission",
    "content_id": "pg_41fb941ec5dc",
    "creator_id": "readme_test",
    "content_type": "text",
    "verdict": "likely_ai",
    "confidence": 81,
    "p_ai": 0.811,
    "signals": { "llm": 0.8, "stylometric": 0.705, "lexical": 1.0 }
  },
  {
    "timestamp": "2026-07-01T04:46:35+00:00",
    "event": "appeal",
    "content_id": "pg_41fb941ec5dc",
    "creator_id": "readme_test",
    "status": "under_review",
    "appeal_reasoning": "I wrote this myself for a philosophy class; happy to share drafts.",
    "original_verdict": "likely_ai",
    "original_confidence": 81
  }
]
```

(All three signals contributed to this decision — `llm` 0.80, `stylometric` 0.705,
`lexical` 1.0. If `GROQ_API_KEY` is unset, `llm` is `null`, the two heuristics carry the
verdict, and the entry is flagged `degraded`.)

## Appeals — handling

`POST /appeal` with `{content_id, creator_reasoning}` looks up the submission, sets its
status to `under_review`, appends an appeal entry beside the original decision, and
returns a confirmation. **No automated re-classification** — a human reviewer reads the
queue via `GET /log`. See the appeal entry above.

## Stretch features (all four)

- **Ensemble detection** — a **third** signal (lexical) joins the LLM + stylometric
  pair with a documented `0.50 / 0.30 / 0.20` weighting (see Detection signals).
- **Provenance certificate** — `POST /verify` lets a creator earn a **Verified Human**
  credential by completing an attestation step; credentialed creators' content shows the
  🔵 badge. The badge is display-only and deliberately **does not feed the detector**, so
  it can't be abused to launder AI text past the signals.
- **Analytics dashboard** — `GET /dashboard` (HTML) + `GET /analytics` (JSON) show
  verdict breakdown, **appeal rate**, average `p_ai`, signal-disagreement rate,
  verified-human count, and content-type mix.
- **Multimodal support** — `POST /submit` accepts `content_type: "image"` with
  `metadata` + optional `caption`; an image-metadata signal scores generation-tool tags
  (Midjourney/DALL·E/etc.), C2PA `ai_generated` flags, and missing camera EXIF, blended
  with caption stylometry. Example run: Midjourney + C2PA metadata → `p_ai 0.90`, Likely AI.

## Known limitations

- **Formal human non-fiction is the system's hardest case.** Academic or legal prose is
  uniform (low burstiness), clean (high regularity), and connective-heavy — exactly the
  properties the stylometric and lexical signals read as AI. In testing, a real
  monetary-policy paragraph scored `p_ai 0.61` (Uncertain). It is *not* falsely branded
  AI (the asymmetric threshold and the LLM signal hold it back), but its confidence is
  lower than it should be. This is inherent to surface heuristics, not a data problem:
  the fix is more weight on semantic signals, not more examples.
- **Lightly-edited AI** (a human touch-up of AI output) sits near the Uncertain/human
  boundary on heuristics alone — the honest failure mode the LLM signal exists to catch.

## Spec reflection

- **How the spec helped:** its instruction to *"decide what 0.5 should mean to a user
  before figuring out how to achieve it"* forced me to define the asymmetric thresholds
  and the meaning of `p_ai` **before** writing scoring code. That single decision shaped
  the labels, the appeal path, and the false-positive posture — everything downstream.
- **How my implementation diverged:** the spec's default pairing is two signals; I built
  **three** (ensemble stretch) and made the LLM signal gracefully **optional** — when no
  key is present the system degrades to the heuristics and marks the result `degraded`
  rather than failing. That wasn't in the spec, but it made the whole system testable
  end-to-end without a key and more robust to Groq outages in "production."

## AI usage

1. **Scoring calibration.** I directed Claude to combine three signal scores into one
   `p_ai` and implement the three-band label mapping to my spec's thresholds. Its first
   draft used a symmetric `0.5` cutoff; I **overrode** it to the asymmetric `0.72 / 0.40`
   thresholds so a human is harder to accuse than to clear, and re-tested that formal
   human prose stays out of the "Likely AI" band.
2. **Stylometric signal + bug fix.** I asked Claude to compute burstiness, type-token
   ratio, and punctuation regularity. Its regularity metric checked the last character
   of each sentence — but the sentence splitter had already stripped terminal
   punctuation, so the metric was silently always ~0. I caught it by printing the
   component breakdown, and rewrote it to measure informal-marker density instead, which
   moved clearly-AI text from `0.46 → 0.70` on that component.

---

## Repo contents

```
app.py            # Flask app + all endpoints (/submit /appeal /log /verify /analytics /dashboard)
signals.py        # detection signals: llm, stylometric, lexical, image_metadata
scoring.py        # weighted combine + asymmetric thresholds + display confidence
labels.py         # the three transparency-label variants + verified badge
storage.py        # structured JSON persistence (audit log, submissions, creators)
analytics.py      # dashboard metrics
templates/dashboard.html
planning.md       # design spec (written before implementation)
requirements.txt  .env.example  .gitignore
```

## How to run

```bash
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/Scripts/activate      # Windows (Git Bash);  macOS/Linux: source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

cp .env.example .env               # then put your Groq key in .env  (GROQ_API_KEY=...)
python app.py                      # http://localhost:5000
```

Try it:

```bash
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:5000/submit \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"text":"The sun dipped below the horizon, painting the sky in amber and rose.","creator_id":"you"}'

# copy the content_id from the response, then appeal it:
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:5000/appeal \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"content_id":"PASTE_ID","creator_reasoning":"I wrote this myself."}'

curl -s http://localhost:5000/log        # see the structured audit log
```

> Runs **without** a key (heuristics only, results flagged `degraded`); add
> `GRO
